expanded_description: The slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines follow precise algebraic relationships: parallel lines have identical slopes, and perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals whose product equals $-1$. These relationships provide the algebraic foundation for analyzing and constructing parallel and perpendicular lines in the coordinate plane, connecting the visual geometric concepts of parallelism and perpendicularity to their numerical slope values.

core_concept.principle: Two distinct non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if their slopes are equal: $m_1 = m_2$. Two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if their slopes are negative reciprocals: $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$, or equivalently $m_2 = -\frac{1}{m_1}$. These algebraic conditions are the coordinate geometry equivalents of the geometric definitions of parallel and perpendicular lines.
core_concept.explanation: Think of slope as the 'DNA' of a line's direction. Parallel lines have identical DNA—they tilt the exact same way, so they never meet. Perpendicular lines have DNA that are 'opposite and flipped'—one rises while the other falls at a perfectly complementary steepness. If one line goes up 2 for every 1 right, a perpendicular line goes down 1 for every 2 right. The product $-1$ relationship is a perfect mathematical expression of the right angle between them. These slope rules transform geometric intuition into algebraic certainty—you can prove lines are parallel or perpendicular just by calculating and comparing numbers.

image.alt: Two pairs of lines on coordinate planes: (1) Two parallel lines with identical slope triangles showing $m=2$ for both, labeled $m_1=m_2=2$. (2) Two perpendicular lines intersecting at a right angle, one with $m=3$ and the other with $m=-\frac{1}{3}$, labeled $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$. Both relationships displayed as formulas.
image.caption: Parallel lines: $m_1 = m_2$. Perpendicular lines: $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$ (negative reciprocals).

sections.introduction: Parallel lines never intersect and always maintain the same distance apart. Algebraically, this geometric property translates into a simple rule: parallel lines have exactly the same slope. Every distinct non-vertical line with slope $m$ has infinitely many parallel lines, all sharing that same slope but differing in their $y$-intercepts.

introduction_image.alt: A coordinate plane showing three parallel lines: $y=2x+1$, $y=2x-3$, and $y=2x+4$. All three have identical slope triangles showing $m=2$. Their different $y$-intercepts are circled at $(0,1)$, $(0,-3)$, and $(0,4)$. Arrows indicate they never intersect.
introduction_image.caption: A family of parallel lines: all have $m=2$, but different $y$-intercepts. Same slope = parallel.

key_concept.definition: Parallel Lines Slope Theorem: Two distinct non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if they have the same slope. If $m_1 = m_2$, the lines are parallel (or coincident if they also share a point). All vertical lines are parallel to each other but have undefined slopes, so this theorem applies only to non-vertical lines.
key_concept.context: This theorem is the algebraic equivalent of the Euclidean parallel postulates studied in Sections 3.1-3.5. It allows parallelism to be determined purely from coordinates or equations without drawing diagrams or measuring angles. If two lines have different slopes, they must intersect at exactly one point.

image.alt: A comprehensive diagram: Two parallel lines with equations $y=mx+b_1$ and $y=mx+b_2$. Same slope $m$, different intercepts. Slope triangles on both lines show identical $\frac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}}$. The condition $m_1=m_2$ is displayed prominently. A note: 'If $b_1=b_2$, the lines are coincident (the same line).'
image.caption: Parallel lines have equal slopes. Different $y$-intercepts guarantee they are distinct.

process_flow.process: Determine the Slope of Each Line
process_flow.description: Find the slope of both lines. Slopes can be read from slope-intercept form, calculated from two points, or determined from the graph.
process_flow.mechanism: If equations are in slope-intercept form $y = mx + b$, the slope is the coefficient $m$. If given two points, use $m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$. If given a graph, count the rise and run between two points. For vertical lines, note that the slope is undefined.
process_flow.result: Numerical slope values $m_1$ and $m_2$ for the two lines.

image.alt: Three representations of lines: (1) Equation $y=3x-5$ with $m=3$ highlighted. (2) Two points with slope calculation. (3) A graphed line with slope triangle. All three methods converging on slope values $m_1$ and $m_2$.
image.caption: Step 1: Find the slope of each line using the most convenient method.

process_flow.description: Check whether the slopes are equal. If they are, the lines are parallel (or coincident). If they are not equal, the lines intersect.
process_flow.mechanism: If $m_1 = m_2$ and the lines are distinct (different $y$-intercepts or different points), the lines are parallel. If $m_1 = m_2$ and the lines share a point, they are the same line (coincident). If $m_1 \neq m_2$, the lines intersect at exactly one point. Vertical lines require special handling: all vertical lines are parallel to each other.
process_flow.result: A determination of whether the lines are parallel, coincident, or intersecting.

image.alt: A decision flowchart: $m_1 = m_2$? → Yes → Same $y$-intercept? → Yes → Same line (coincident). → No → Distinct parallel lines. $m_1 = m_2$? → No → Lines intersect. Examples of each outcome with graphs.
image.caption: Step 2: Compare slopes. Equal slopes → parallel or coincident. Different slopes → intersect.

process_flow.description: Declare whether the lines are parallel and cite the slope equality as the reason.
process_flow.mechanism: If $m_1 = m_2$, state that the lines are parallel by the Parallel Lines Slope Theorem (or by the fact that equal slopes imply parallel lines). If using a proof, cite the theorem: 'Lines with equal slopes are parallel.' If the slopes differ, state that the lines are not parallel and will intersect.
process_flow.result: A clear geometric conclusion supported by algebraic evidence.
applications: 'Both lines are vertical, and all vertical lines are parallel.'
'$m_1 \neq m_2$, so the lines intersect and are not parallel.'

image.alt: Two parallel lines with matching slope triangles and the equation $m_1=m_2$ displayed. A conclusion statement: 'Lines are parallel — slopes are equal.' The Parallel Lines Slope Theorem is cited.
image.caption: Step 3: Conclude parallelism, citing the equal slopes as justification.

classifications.value: Lines with equal slopes may be either distinct parallel lines or the same line. Checking a point distinguishes them.
characteristics: Same slope + different $y$-intercepts → distinct parallel lines (never intersect)
Same slope + same $y$-intercept → coincident lines (the same line, infinitely many intersection points)
Same slope + share any point → coincident (they are the same line)
Different slopes → intersecting lines (exactly one intersection point)
classifications.behavior: Two lines with identical equations are the same line. Two lines with the same slope but different equations are parallel. Always check whether the lines are truly distinct before claiming they are parallel.

examples.explanation:
$y = 2x + 3$ and $y = 2x - 5$: $m_1 = m_2 = 2$, intercepts $3 \neq -5$ → distinct parallel lines.

$y = 2x + 3$ and $2y = 4x + 6$: Simplify second: $y = 2x + 3$ → same equation → coincident (same line, not 'parallel').

Always simplify both equations fully before comparing.

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) Distinct parallel lines — same slope, different intercepts. (2) Coincident lines — single line with two equivalent equations. (3) Intersecting lines — different slopes, cross at one point. Each labeled with conditions.
image.caption: Same slope + different intercepts = parallel. Same slope + same intercept = coincident. Different slopes = intersect.

image.alt: Complete section summary: the Parallel Lines Slope Theorem, the three-step process, the parallel/coincident/intersecting classification, and examples with various slope comparisons.
image.caption: Slopes of Parallel Lines overview: equal slopes define parallelism in the coordinate plane.

sections.introduction: Perpendicular lines intersect at right angles. Algebraically, this means their slopes have a special relationship: they are negative reciprocals whose product is always $-1$. This elegant formula captures the geometry of perpendicularity in a single numerical condition.

introduction_image.alt: Two perpendicular lines intersecting at a right angle (marked with square). One line has slope $m_1 = \frac{3}{2}$, the other has slope $m_2 = -\frac{2}{3}$. Slope triangles on both lines illustrate the negative reciprocal relationship. Equation displayed: $m_1 \cdot m_2 = \frac{3}{2} \cdot (-\frac{2}{3}) = -1$.
introduction_image.caption: Perpendicular lines: slopes are negative reciprocals. Product always equals $-1$.

key_concept.definition: Perpendicular Lines Slope Theorem: Two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if their slopes are negative reciprocals. That is, $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$, or equivalently $m_2 = -\frac{1}{m_1}$. Additionally, a horizontal line ($m = 0$) and a vertical line (undefined slope) are always perpendicular.
key_concept.context: This theorem is the algebraic equivalent of the geometric definition of perpendicular lines. It provides a purely numerical test for perpendicularity: multiply the slopes—if the product is $-1$, the lines are perpendicular. This is widely used in coordinate geometry proofs and in constructing perpendicular lines through given points.

image.alt: Comprehensive diagram: Two perpendicular lines with slopes $m_1$ and $m_2$. The negative reciprocal relationship illustrated: $m_2 = -\frac{1}{m_1}$. Examples: if $m_1=2$, then $m_2=-\frac{1}{2}$; if $m_1=-\frac{3}{4}$, then $m_2=\frac{4}{3}$; if $m_1=1$, then $m_2=-1$. Each pair shown with product $-1$. Horizontal/vertical pair also noted.
image.caption: Perpendicular lines: slopes multiply to $-1$. Horizontal ($m=0$) and vertical (undefined) are also perpendicular.

process_flow.process: Find the Slopes of Both Lines
process_flow.description: Determine $m_1$ and $m_2$ from equations, points, or graphs. Ensure both lines are non-vertical (unless checking the horizontal-vertical case).
process_flow.mechanism: Extract slopes from $y = mx + b$ form, calculate from two points, or read from the graph. If either line is vertical, the slope is undefined—check if the other is horizontal ($m = 0$). If one is vertical and the other horizontal, they are perpendicular. If both are non-vertical, proceed to the multiplication test.
process_flow.result: Slope values $m_1$ and $m_2$, or identification of horizontal/vertical cases.
applications: Line A: horizontal $y = 6$ → $m_A = 0$. Line B: vertical $x = 2$ → $m_B$ undefined. These are perpendicular.

image.alt: Three representations: (1) Equation $y=4x+1$ with $m=4$ highlighted. (2) Two points with slope calculation yielding $-1/4$. (3) Horizontal ($m=0$) and vertical (undefined) lines. All showing slope determination methods.
image.caption: Step 1: Find the slope of each line. Handle horizontal/vertical cases separately.

process_flow.description: Multiply the slopes. If the product equals $-1$, the lines are perpendicular. Equivalently, check if one slope is the negative reciprocal of the other.
process_flow.mechanism: Compute $m_1 \cdot m_2$. If the result is exactly $-1$, the lines are perpendicular. Verify by checking $m_2 = -\frac{1}{m_1}$ or $m_1 = -\frac{1}{m_2}$. Both conditions are equivalent. If the product is NOT $-1$, the lines are not perpendicular (they intersect at a non-right angle).
process_flow.result: A determination of perpendicularity based on the product of slopes.
applications: $m_1=\frac{2}{3}$, $m_2=\frac{3}{2}$ → $\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{2} = 1 \neq -1$ → NOT perpendicular (these are reciprocals but not NEGATIVE reciprocals).

image.alt: The multiplication test illustrated: $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$? A calculator-style display shows three examples: (1) $4 \cdot (-1/4) = -1$ → YES. (2) $(2/3) \cdot (3/2) = 1$ → NO (reciprocals but not negative). (3) $2 \cdot (-2) = -4$ → NO (negatives but not reciprocals).
image.caption: Step 2: Multiply the slopes. Product = $-1$? Perpendicular. Otherwise, not.

process_flow.description: Declare whether the lines are perpendicular, citing the slope product rule as justification.
process_flow.mechanism: If $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$, state that the lines are perpendicular by the Perpendicular Lines Slope Theorem. If one slope is $0$ and the other is undefined, state that horizontal and vertical lines are perpendicular. If neither condition holds, state that the lines are not perpendicular.
process_flow.result: A clear geometric conclusion justified by the algebraic slope relationship.
applications: 'Line A is horizontal and Line B is vertical, so they are perpendicular.'
'$m_1 \cdot m_2 = 2 \neq -1$, so the lines are not perpendicular.'

image.alt: Two perpendicular lines with right angle marked. The equation $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$ displayed. A conclusion statement: 'Lines are perpendicular — slopes are negative reciprocals.' The Perpendicular Lines Slope Theorem is cited.
image.caption: Step 3: Conclude perpendicularity, citing the negative reciprocal relationship as justification.

classifications.value: Understanding what makes two numbers negative reciprocals is essential for the perpendicular test.
characteristics: To find the negative reciprocal: flip the fraction and change the sign
$m$ and $-\frac{1}{m}$ are negative reciprocals; their product is $-1$
The slope $0$ has no reciprocal (division by zero); its 'perpendicular partner' is an undefined slope (vertical line)
classifications.behavior: The negative reciprocal operation is two steps: (1) take the reciprocal (flip numerator and denominator), (2) change the sign. The order doesn't matter—flip then negate, or negate then flip. The result is the same.

examples.explanation:
Given slope $m = \frac{3}{4}$:

Step 1: Reciprocal → $\frac{4}{3}$.

Step 2: Change sign → $-\frac{4}{3}$.

The perpendicular slope is $-\frac{4}{3}$.

Check: $\frac{3}{4} \cdot (-\frac{4}{3}) = -\frac{12}{12} = -1$ ✓.

image.alt: Four examples of negative reciprocal pairs: (1) $2$ → $-1/2$. (2) $-3$ → $1/3$. (3) $4/5$ → $-5/4$. (4) $1$ → $-1$. Each shown with the flip-and-negate operation and the product $-1$ verified.
image.caption: Negative reciprocals: flip the fraction and change the sign. Product always $-1$.

image.alt: Complete section summary: the Perpendicular Lines Slope Theorem, the three-step verification process, negative reciprocal examples, and the horizontal/vertical special case.
image.caption: Slopes of Perpendicular Lines overview: negative reciprocals, product $-1$, and the horizontal/vertical exception.

sections.section_title: Comparing Parallel and Perpendicular Slope Relationships
sections.introduction: Parallel and perpendicular lines have distinct slope relationships that are often confused. Placing them side by side clarifies the difference: parallel lines share identical slopes, while perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals. Knowing when to set slopes equal and when to multiply to $-1$ is a key skill.

introduction_image.alt: Side-by-side comparison: Left — two parallel lines with matching slope triangles, equation $m_1 = m_2$. Right — two perpendicular lines with right angle, equation $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$. A question mark between them: 'Which relationship applies?'
introduction_image.caption: Parallel: $m_1 = m_2$. Perpendicular: $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$. Two distinct algebraic signatures.

key_concept.definition: Parallel lines: $m_1 = m_2$ (equal slopes). Perpendicular lines: $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$ (negative reciprocals). These are the two fundamental slope relationships. They cannot both hold simultaneously for distinct lines—if lines are parallel, they cannot be perpendicular, and vice versa.
key_concept.context: These two relationships are the algebraic foundation for coordinate geometry proofs involving parallel and perpendicular lines. They are used to classify quadrilaterals (parallelograms, rectangles, rhombuses, squares) and to solve construction problems (find a line parallel or perpendicular to a given line through a point).

image.alt: A reference table: Row 1 — Parallel Lines: Condition $m_1=m_2$, Example $m_1=3$, $m_2=3$, Visual shows matching slope triangles. Row 2 — Perpendicular Lines: Condition $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$, Example $m_1=2$, $m_2=-1/2$, Visual shows right angle. Row 3 — Neither: Lines intersect but not at 90°, slopes are neither equal nor negative reciprocals.
image.caption: Reference: Parallel = equal slopes. Perpendicular = negative reciprocals. Neither = all other slope pairs.

worked_examples.title: Classifying Line Pairs Using Slopes
worked_examples.problem:
Determine whether each pair of lines is parallel, perpendicular, or neither.

1) Line 1: $y = \frac{2}{5}x + 3$. Line 2: $y = \frac{2}{5}x - 7$.
2) Line 1: $y = 4x - 1$. Line 2: $y = -\frac{1}{4}x + 2$.
3) Line 1: through $(0, 0)$ and $(3, 6)$. Line 2: through $(0, 0)$ and $(6, -3)$.
4) Line 1: $y = 5$. Line 2: $x = -3$.

step_by_step.explanation:
$m_1 = \frac{2}{5}$, $m_2 = \frac{2}{5}$. Slopes are equal.

$b_1 = 3 \neq -7 = b_2$. Different intercepts.

Conclusion: PARALLEL (distinct lines, same slope).

Line 1: $m_1 = \frac{6-0}{3-0} = \frac{6}{3} = 2$.

Line 2: $m_2 = \frac{-3-0}{6-0} = \frac{-3}{6} = -\frac{1}{2}$.

$m_1 \cdot m_2 = 2 \cdot (-\frac{1}{2}) = -1$.

Conclusion: PERPENDICULAR (both through origin — they form an X with right angles).

Line 1: $y = 5$ → horizontal, $m_1 = 0$.

Line 2: $x = -3$ → vertical, slope undefined.

Horizontal and vertical lines are PERPENDICULAR.

image.alt: Four pairs of lines graphed: (1) Two parallel lines with $m=2/5$. (2) Perpendicular lines with slopes $4$ and $-1/4$. (3) Lines through origin with slopes $2$ and $-1/2$, perpendicular. (4) Horizontal $y=5$ and vertical $x=-3$, perpendicular.
image.caption: Classifying line pairs: parallel (equal slopes), perpendicular (product $-1$), or neither.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Parallel ($m_1 = m_2 = 2/5$, different intercepts).
2) Perpendicular ($4 \cdot (-1/4) = -1$).
3) Perpendicular ($2 \cdot (-1/2) = -1$).
4) Perpendicular (horizontal and vertical).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Parallel slopes are equal. Perpendicular slopes multiply to $-1$. Horizontal and vertical lines are perpendicular.

sections.section_title: Using Slope Relationships to Construct Lines
sections.introduction: The slope relationships for parallel and perpendicular lines make it possible to construct equations of lines with these properties. Given a line and a point, you can write the equation of a parallel or perpendicular line through that point—a skill essential for coordinate geometry proofs and applications.

introduction_image.alt: A given line $L$ with slope $m=2$. A point $P(3,5)$ not on $L$. Two constructed lines through $P$: a parallel line (same slope $m=2$, equation $y-5=2(x-3)$) and a perpendicular line (slope $m=-1/2$, equation $y-5=-\frac{1}{2}(x-3)$). All three lines graphed.
introduction_image.caption: Constructing lines: parallel line uses the same slope. Perpendicular line uses the negative reciprocal.

key_concept.definition: To construct a line parallel to a given line through a point $P(x_1, y_1)$: use the same slope $m$ and write $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$. To construct a line perpendicular to a given line through $P$: use the negative reciprocal slope $-\frac{1}{m}$ and write $y - y_1 = -\frac{1}{m}(x - x_1)$. Both use point-slope form with the appropriate slope.
key_concept.context: This construction skill is applied in coordinate geometry to prove quadrilaterals are parallelograms (opposite sides parallel), rectangles (adjacent sides perpendicular), and to find altitudes of triangles and perpendicular bisectors of segments.

image.alt: A reference card: Left — constructing a parallel line (copy the slope, use point-slope form). Right — constructing a perpendicular line (use negative reciprocal slope, point-slope form). Both with step-by-step algebraic work and final equations.
image.caption: Constructing parallel/perpendicular lines: determine the appropriate slope, then apply point-slope form.

worked_examples.title: Constructing Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
worked_examples.problem:
1) Write the equation of the line parallel to $y = 3x - 4$ that passes through $(1, 5)$.

2) Write the equation of the line perpendicular to $y = -\frac{2}{5}x + 1$ that passes through $(-3, 4)$.

3) A line passes through $(2, 1)$ and $(6, 9)$. Write the equation of the line perpendicular to it that passes through the midpoint of the segment.

step_by_step.explanation:
Slope of given line: $m = \frac{9-1}{6-2} = \frac{8}{4} = 2$.

Perpendicular slope: $m_{\perp} = -\frac{1}{2}$.

Midpoint: $(\frac{2+6}{2}, \frac{1+9}{2}) = (4, 5)$.

Equation through midpoint: $y - 5 = -\frac{1}{2}(x - 4)$.

Slope-intercept: $y = -\frac{1}{2}x + 7$.

image.alt: Three graphs: (1) Original line $y=3x-4$ and parallel line $y=3x+2$. (2) Original line $y=-2/5 x+1$ and perpendicular line $y=5/2 x+23/2$ with right angle. (3) Segment from (2,1) to (6,9), its midpoint (4,5), and the perpendicular bisector $y=-1/2 x+7$.
image.caption: Constructing parallel and perpendicular lines through given points.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Parallel lines use identical slopes. Perpendicular lines use negative reciprocal slopes. Point-slope form with the appropriate slope constructs the desired line.

sections.section_title: Practice: Slopes of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines
sections.introduction: Mastery of parallel and perpendicular slope relationships requires practice identifying relationships, classifying line pairs, finding negative reciprocals, and constructing equations. Work through these problems to build comprehensive fluency.

worked_examples.title: Parallel and Perpendicular Slopes Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Find the negative reciprocal of each slope: (a) $5$, (b) $-\frac{3}{7}$, (c) $1$, (d) $-2$.
2) Line 1: $y = \frac{1}{3}x + 4$. Line 2: $y = -3x - 2$. Parallel, perpendicular, or neither?
3) Line 1: through $(2, 5)$ and $(4, 9)$. Line 2: through $(1, 8)$ and $(3, 12)$. Parallel, perpendicular, or neither?
4) Write the equation of the line parallel to $y = -x + 6$ through $(3, -2)$.
5) Write the equation of the line perpendicular to $y = \frac{5}{8}x - 1$ through $(0, 0)$.

step_by_step.explanation:
(a) $5 = \frac{5}{1}$ → negative reciprocal: $-\frac{1}{5}$.
(b) $-\frac{3}{7}$ → negative reciprocal: $\frac{7}{3}$.
(c) $1 = \frac{1}{1}$ → negative reciprocal: $-1$.
(d) $-2 = -\frac{2}{1}$ → negative reciprocal: $\frac{1}{2}$.

image.alt: Five panels: (1) Negative reciprocal examples. (2) Perpendicular lines $y=1/3 x+4$ and $y=-3x-2$. (3) Parallel lines through four points. (4) Parallel line $y=-x+1$. (5) Perpendicular line $y=-8/5 x$ through origin.
image.caption: Parallel and perpendicular slopes practice: negative reciprocals, classification, and line construction.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Negative reciprocals, parallel/perpendicular classification from equations and points, and constructing lines with specified relationships.

summary: The slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines follow precise algebraic relationships. Key points: 1) Parallel Lines: Two distinct non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if $m_1 = m_2$. Equal slopes guarantee parallelism (or coincidence if the lines are the same). All vertical lines are parallel. 2) Perpendicular Lines: Two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$, meaning their slopes are negative reciprocals. Horizontal ($m=0$) and vertical (undefined slope) lines are also perpendicular. 3) Negative Reciprocals: To find the perpendicular slope, flip the fraction and change the sign. $m$ and $-\frac{1}{m}$ are negative reciprocals; their product is always $-1$. 4) Classification: Equal slopes → parallel. Product $-1$ → perpendicular. Neither condition → intersecting but not perpendicular. 5) Construction: To write the equation of a parallel line, use the same slope with point-slope form. To write the equation of a perpendicular line, use the negative reciprocal slope with point-slope form. 6) These algebraic relationships are the coordinate geometry equivalents of the geometric parallel and perpendicular postulates and theorems, enabling precise numerical analysis of line relationships.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Slopes of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines' in center. Branches: Parallel Lines ($m_1=m_2$, same slope, different intercepts for distinct lines, vertical lines are parallel), Perpendicular Lines ($m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$, negative reciprocals, horizontal ⊥ vertical), Negative Reciprocal (flip fraction, change sign, product always $-1$), Classification (parallel/perpendicular/neither), Construction (parallel: use same $m$, perpendicular: use $-1/m$, apply point-slope form).
review_questions_image.caption: Slopes of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines Review: the algebraic relationships that define parallelism and perpendicularity in the coordinate plane.
